author: @kokorosenshi
text: if you need parts of a print to be conductive, you might benefit from a dual extrusion fdm 3d printer. You'll be able to use two different types of spools. One, ABS or PLA for the main body of the print and the second spool that is either conductive or graphene infused. You can take a normal 3d printer and convert it to a dual extrusion 3d printer but it will reduce the printing space.

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, try silver nanoparticles instead of graphite for conductive resin. The silver nanoparticles may let more UV light into the part to cure it.

author: @svenp6504
text: Re: conductivity, tin oxide and indium-tin oxide are conductive (often used for making plastics and glass conductive) and optically transparent, though I'm not sure if they would be in the UV range.  If they are transparent in the UV range then you wouldn't run into the issue of the resin not setting.

author: @TeslaFactory
text: What about metal nanoparticles? Could decrease the amount of graphene needed

author: @-robo-
text:
Conductive: what if you added to your design "channels" where the graphene could be painted once the print is done. Just deep enough so the working ink won't rub off.  Perhaps the channels could be in between connecting parts so it is is protected (embedded) once the final model is assembled. If the model doesn't  have multiple parts, design in small tunnels were working ink could be pushed through via air pressure. The net effect would be "paint in" wiring. (You can say it. It's OK. I believe the word is brilliant.)

Fumes:  build a fume hood you goof lol! You certainly have the skill and I would hazard a guess you have the parts. I  have my resin printer in a DIY enclosure that vents directly outside. I took a $10 plastic shelf kit and covered it with winterizing window plastic (shrinks with heat, crystal clear). The only tricky bit was the door, and I just 3D printed a frame and hinges and used more of window wrap. I use regular dryer hose and a PC fan to move the air. I cut a panel just wider than the vent to fit my window. When I need to use the resin printer, I simply close the window against the board and I am ready to go. Things I plan to upgrade: 1. weather stripping around the board for a better seal. 2. a stronger fan so the enclosure can be used as a fume hood when the door is open. 3. idk yet, but there is always room for improvement.

author: @sonnyhayes689
text:
Hi Rob... I have the same exact 3D printer and am also trying to make the material conductive for commercial product. I am using some ultra-milled carbon fiber (same type mill you use for graphite) and have some success with conductivity while resin is liquid. My percentages are too high allowing the 1.83 specific gravity CF to settle out quickly. Experimenting with various percentage of CF mixes, then curing on an open flat surface and using a set of 385nm LED lights which only cures a very thin layer, approximately 0.05mm in depth after about 2 minutes. Have not yet tried to print using my Elegoo Mar 2 Pro, that sould happen soon. In the menatime continuing to test on flat surfaces. 
Would be happy to send you some CF sample material to test at your side. Warm regards, Leo

author: @scotttovey
text:
Even if you can't make the resin conductive, you can use it to print out circuits and then coat the printed circuit with conductive ink.

Getting the conductive resin to work, perhaps you need to look at the see through solar cell technology and see how they managed to create those.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: perhaps using AZO dispersed in the water washable polymer?

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThomasAndersonbsf 

"I was thinking he just needs to do thinner layers"

There are limits to how thin a 3D printer can print.
Everything has it's limits.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
"perhaps using AZO dispersed in the water washable polymer?"

Possibly.
But you may find that the only way to succeed in a conductive 3D print is with the filament type of printer.

With that method, you would need to make a filament that contains your conductive ink. There are videos on  YouTube that show how to build a filament extruder. 

If you decide to try that route keep in mind that those printer systems don't work so well when the environment is dusty, so if your shop has a tendency to build up dust, make sure you build a cabinet for the printer or purchase a printer that comes with one.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThomasAndersonbsf 


"atoms are your limit, when it comes to layer thickness,"

You're joking right?
The machine that Robert has is not capable of doing atomic layering. In fact, there's not a 3D printer on the market that can do a layer that thin.

The only Atomic moving that gets done is in a lab that has a scanning tunneling microscope (STM) which has an ultrasharp tip. 

You can get a single and trilayer Graphene, but that tech is still in it's infancy and is not available in a 3D printer as it took 1,510°C and 1,300°C degree temperatures to achieve.

The thickness of a layer may not have anything to do with the motors, but certainly, a stepper that has more than 280 steps per rotation would aid in achieving thinner layers.

You also have an issue with the slicing software. The better the slicer, the better the layers will come out, but it takes developing a machine and the software together, in order to achieve the finner layers. You can't always just change the controller and the slicer.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThomasAndersonbsf 
"why are you insisting on telling people what they can't do?"

Why are you insisting on misconstruing what people say and living in a delusion?

I said the technology is not at the stage of being able to do it. I didn't say it could not be done. There is a big difference between the two.

If you think it can be done with current affordable technology, then purchase the tech, do it and then show the world that you have done it.

Regardless of whether you do it or not; stop whining about people stating the reality of the limitations of technology. 

This is not a Harry Potter hocus pocus fantasy film. This is reality. The only way to get past the limitations of technology is to develop and engineer new technology that surpasses the limitations of current technology.

author: @saiello2061
text: As I'm sure you're aware, conductive graphene infused PLA filament already exists. Unfortunately it can be horrendously expensive at over £1000 per Kg for the "good" stuff or cheap and not so good at conducting a current. If you can come up with a good and cheap way to produce it then that'd be amazing! 👍 On the other hand, if you wanted a guarenteed way to make a printed part as conductive as metal you could always plate it 😁.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @saiello2061
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering No worries, I always write my comments as if for a general audience, I would never assume you didn't know more than most about everything graphene... 😁 Look forward to your investigative inroads... 👍
